task: I’m thinking about picking up a used dirt bike part from someone local, but I don’t want to waste a trip if it’s usually a bad sign. What are the main things you’d look for before meeting up, and what would make you walk away?

turns.agent_response:
Before meeting up, I’d try to confirm a few things so you don’t waste a trip:

- **Exact fitment**: year/make/model, and if possible a part number or clear photo of the part installed
- **Reason for selling**: upgrade, leftover from a build, bike sold, etc. Better than “not sure”
- **Clear photos**: all sides, close-ups of mounting points, threads, seals, splines, bends, cracks, welds, and any wear surfaces
- **Condition specifics**: ask directly what’s wrong with it, if anything
- **How it was used**: trail bike, race bike, crash damage, submerged, sitting outside, etc.
- **Whether it’s complete**: hardware, bushings, bearings, spacers, covers, etc.
- **Price vs. risk**: if it’s a critical part, cheap isn’t always worth it if there’s any doubt

I’d **walk away** if:
- They won’t send clear pictures
- The story doesn’t make sense or keeps changing
- The part is **bent, cracked, welded, or heavily corroded**
- Mounting holes, threads, or splines are damaged
- It’s a **safety/critical part** and they can’t verify it’s straight and functional
- They push for a fast meet with no questions answered

For dirt bike parts, I’d be extra cautious with:
- **Wheels/rims**
- **Triple clamps**
- **Forks/shock**
- **Handlebars**
- **Brake components**
- **Swingarm/frame-related parts**

If you want, send me the part you’re looking at and I can tell you the specific red flags to check for on that one.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, mostly. But I’d want it tighter than that.

Before I even drive out:
- **Ask for the exact bike it came off of**. Year/make/model, not just “should fit most 125s” or whatever.
- **Look for weird vagueness**. If they can’t say where it came from, that’s a bad sign.
- **Check the pics hard** for cracks, bends, stripped threads, rounded bolts, chewed-up mounts.
- **If it’s a part that takes a hit** — forks, rims, bars, clamps, brake stuff — I’d be way more picky.
- **If they only have one blurry pic**, I’d probably pass. Not worth burning gas.

I’d walk if:
- they won’t say what bike it’s from
- the price is stupid low for no reason
- they rush the meet like they don’t want questions
- the part looks sprayed over, welded, or “fixed” with sketchy stuff
- it’s anything safety-related and they can’t prove it’s straight

Last time I ignored a sketchy deal, it turned into a headache real fast. Not doing that again.

If you want, I can give you a quick red-flag list for one specific part — like fork, wheel, carb, plastics, whatever.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — for a **local used dirt bike part**, I’d treat it like this:

## Before you drive out, I’d confirm:
- **Exact bike it came off of**: year / make / model / submodel
- **Exact part**: not just “front end” or “some 250 stuff”
- **Clear photos of the actual item**:
  - all sides
  - mounting points
  - threads / bolt holes
  - any wear surfaces
  - close-ups of damage
- **Reason it’s being sold**
- **Whether it’s complete**: bushings, spacers, bearings, hardware, seals, etc.
- **Whether it was crashed, submerged, or repaired**
- **Any known issues**: bent, cracked, leaks, missing pieces

## Big red flags I’d usually walk away on:
- **Won’t tell you what bike it came off of**
- **Only blurry or obviously cropped photos**
- **Vague answers** like “should fit a bunch of bikes”
- **Spray paint, fresh grease, or “refinished” look** hiding damage
- **Bent, cracked, welded, or repaired sketchily**
- **Stripped threads, chewed bolts, rounded mounts**
- **Rust/corrosion on critical surfaces**
- **Any sign it took a hard hit** on parts like:
  - forks
  - rims
  - triple clamps
  - handlebars
  - brake parts
  - swingarm / frame-related stuff

## For me, the quickest “don’t bother” signs are:
- wrong or unknown fitment
- no clear pics
- seller is evasive
- the part looks damaged or “fixed up”
- it’s safety-critical and they can’t prove it’s straight

If you want, I can make you a **super short checklist by part type** — like forks, wheel, carb, engine part, plastics — so you can decide in 30 seconds whether to make the trip.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s tighter. Pretty much the right filter.

The only thing I’d add is **don’t just trust fitment by model name** — some years split weird, and sellers act like every 125 or 250 is the same thing. Ask for the **exact year range and any part number** if there is one.

And if it’s a **moving or load-bearing part**, I’d be extra cold about it:
- forks: check tubes for pitting, leaks, twist
- wheel/rim: true, dents, cracks by spokes
- clamps/bars: bends, hairline cracks, stripped pinch bolts
- brake stuff: no leaks, no seized pistons, no cooked hose
- anything with bearings: no play, no grind

If the seller gets weird about pics or info, I’d just stop there. Usually means something’s off.

That’s basically the whole game: exact bike, exact part, clear pics, no vague crap. If any one of those is missing, it’s probably not worth the gas.
